range.nativeRange: Species native to Iran and Central Asia; 'Purple Sensation' is a Dutch-bred cultivar

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US; ornamental cultivar introduced from Europe/Central Asia; not naturalized in the wild

invasive.description: Not invasive in the US; ornamental allium does not escape cultivation aggressively; note that some Allium species do naturalize but A. hollandicum is not a documented problem

description.spread: 6-8" per bulb; slowly expands by offset
description.lifespan: Long-lived and reliably perennial; naturalizes over time by bulb offsets
description.deciduousEvergreen: Dormant in summer; foliage appears in early spring and begins to yellow and die back while the plant is in bloom — a notable aesthetic consideration (see Pruning Notes)
description.winterInterest: None — fully dormant; dried seedheads can be ornamental if left standing

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun to part shade; tolerates partial shade well
lightAndWater.water: Low to medium; drought tolerant once established

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained preferred; tolerates average; dislikes prolonged wet feet but more forgiving than tulips in clay

claySoilPerformance.description: Moderate; bulbs can rot in waterlogged clay; well-drained soil is important; raised beds or mounded planting help; clay with good surface drainage is often acceptable

soil.texturePreference: Adaptable; tolerates clay better than most ornamental bulbs
soil.rootCharacter: True bulb; produces offsets that slowly expand the planting; self-seeds modestly

bloom.bloomTime: May to June — bridges the gap between spring crocus/tulip season and summer perennial bloom
bloom.bloomDescription: Dense spherical umbels 4-5" in diameter of vivid violet-purple individual florets on tall, stiff, leafless stems; dramatic, architectural presence above the perennial layer
bloom.fragrance: Flowers mildly onion-scented; foliage strongly onion-scented when bruised
bloom.pollination: Excellent pollinator plant — bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects work the flowers heavily

yield.edibleParts: Technically edible (onion family); flowers used as garnish; not typically grown for food harvest
yield.medicinalUses: Allium family broadly has cardiovascular and antimicrobial benefits; not typically used medicinally in this form
yield.craftOtherYield: Dried seedheads for arrangements; excellent dried ornamental

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic to people at normal exposure; edible
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Toxic to dogs and cats — allium family causes hemolytic anemia in dogs and cats if ingested in significant quantity; cats are more sensitive than dogs; bulbs are most concentrated. Not a front-line concern with outdoor planting but worth noting if pets dig in beds.
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: The strongly aromatic foliage suppresses some pests but no documented allelopathy to neighboring plants

roles: Pest Confuser (strongly aromatic foliage masks chemical signals that attract pest insects — one of the most effective role-players in this category)
companionPlants: or echinacea directly in front — their foliage emerges as allium leaves yellow
masking the die-back. Excellent companion to roses (pest confuser effect benefits nearby plants).
antagonists: None documented for garden companions; keep away from beans and peas (alliums may inhibit legume nitrogen fixation)
permaculture.coppiceable: N/A — allow foliage to die back naturally; do not remove until fully brown

wildlife.attracts: Bees (heavily worked), butterflies, hoverflies; dried seedheads eaten by some small birds

deer.description: Excellent — one of the most reliably deer-resistant bulbs available; the onion scent deters deer strongly

tolerances.other: Squirrels and rodents generally avoid allium bulbs (unlike tulips and crocus)

maintenance.pruningNotes: The foliage begins yellowing and dying back while the plant is still in bloom — this is normal and unavoidable. Plant neighboring perennials (geranium 'Rozanne' is ideal) directly in front of allium groupings to mask this. Leave dried seedheads through summer into fall — they are ornamental and provide seeds for birds. Cut back dead foliage in mid-summer once fully brown.
propagationMethods: Bulb offsets (lift in summer, separate, replant)
seed (self-sows lightly; seedlings bloom in 2-3 years)

cultivarNotes: 'Purple Sensation' is the standard selection for deep violet-purple — reliable, vigorous, and widely available. Performs well across Zones 4-9. 'Gladiator' (pale lavender-purple, slightly larger heads, 24-30") and 'Globemaster' (huge 8-10" heads, longer lasting, 24-36" — spectacular but larger) are alternatives. For smaller scale: Allium moly 'Jeannine' (bright yellow, 10-12" — deer resistant, excellent contrast with purple selections). Allium christophii (stars-of-Persia) produces enormous 8-10" silvery-purple globes on 18" stems and dries beautifully in place.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Ornamental border use, naturalistic planting, spring bulb companion; plant in clusters behind and between late-emerging perennials to mask yellowing foliage; the architectural purple globes extend color through the gap between tulip and summer perennial season; also appropriate in herb layers as a pest confuser
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus: To source (order in September for fall planting)

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: Not native to PA; ornamental introduction
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins red clay: plant on a raised mound or in amended soil; avoid low spots; Section A north-facing beds may need grit amendment for reliable drainage
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Hegins: grit amendment helpful in clay; Section A beds benefit from compost + grit mix
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: 'Purple Sensation' is fully hardy in Zone 6a; reliable performer for Section A ornamental beds
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Section A primary placement; palette match with purple/pink theme; companion with tulips and other spring bulbs; plant mid-zone behind geranium 'Rozanne' groups
